title: Palmshed
summary: Evaluations of command-line AI coding agents based on sustained engineering use.

sections.heading: Palmshed
sections.content: Evaluations of command-line AI coding agents, grounded in sustained engineering use. Tools are organized into three groups based on current use. Active tools are used regularly and their reviews are maintained. Occasional tools are evaluated periodically as they evolve. Archived reviews record observations from past use; the tools are no longer in the current workflow. Assessments shift as tools change, and the site is updated to reflect that.

sections.heading: Ranking
sections.content: Listed by status group, in the order they are currently reached for. All agents with version details and full reviews.

title: About — Palmshed
summary: Thoughtful engineering, honest evaluation, and enduring craft.

sections.heading: About
sections.content: Palmshed exists because building software carefully matters. The conviction behind it is that careful work deserves honest attention, and that truthful assessment serves others better than advocacy. This conviction guides what belongs here. Content grows from sustained engagement with the tools, ideas, and work it describes. The aim is to write carefully about what deserves attention, not to be complete. No observation is final. Understanding deepens. Tools and practices change. The site changes with them. Questions or feedback can be directed to the repository.

title: Ethos — Palmshed
summary: The convictions that govern the work found here.

sections.heading: Ethos
sections.content: How we work reveals what we value. These convictions govern the work found here.

sections.heading: Patience — Why should good work take time?
sections.content: Good work cannot be hurried into existence. It requires sustained attention, repeated revision, and the willingness to set something aside and return to it later. Time is not an obstacle to careful work. It is one of its conditions.

sections.heading: Integrity — Why must the work remain truthful?
sections.content: Truthful work depends on nothing but its own honesty. It does not need persuasion, trends, or marketing to be valuable. The reader is not a target to be convinced but a person to be served. Uncertainty is acknowledged rather than hidden. Limitations are stated plainly. Work that sacrifices truth for effect betrays both the craft and the one who encounters it.

sections.heading: Understanding — Why should observation come before judgment?
sections.content: A first encounter rarely reveals the full character of anything. Judgment without sustained observation is first impression dressed as conclusion. Understanding requires the willingness to let something reveal itself over time, through genuine use rather than controlled demonstration.

sections.heading: Humility — Why should revision never be feared?
sections.content: No observation is final. Understanding deepens. The willingness to revise is not a sign of uncertainty but of honesty. Work that cannot change eventually ceases to tell the truth.

sections.heading: Stewardship — Why is the work never really finished?
sections.content: Anything worth doing requires ongoing attention. Maintenance is not the cost of past decisions. It is the natural extension of care through time. A garden is not maintained because the gardener failed. It is maintained because growth is continuous. The hope is that these convictions become visible through the work itself.

title: Methodology — Palmshed
summary: How CLI coding agent reviews are conducted and classified.

sections.heading: Methodology
sections.content: Every review on this site is based on engineering use rather than feature lists or marketing materials. Agents are evaluated by applying them to real tasks across a range of projects and noting what works, what does not, and where each tool sits in its development. No scores or numerical ratings are assigned. Each agent receives a classification that reflects its current place in the workflow. Because the landscape moves quickly, reviews are updated as tools change and as usage patterns evolve.

sections.heading: Reliability
sections.content: Does the agent produce consistent, correct results? How often does it introduce bugs, hallucinate APIs, or fail to complete tasks?

sections.heading: Context Handling
sections.content: How well does the agent maintain understanding across a conversation or session? Can it reference earlier decisions, track project state, and follow multi-step instructions?

sections.heading: Code Quality
sections.content: Is the generated code idiomatic, maintainable, and consistent with the project's existing style? Does it follow the same conventions a human engineer would?

sections.heading: Repository Understanding
sections.content: How well does the agent grasp the structure, dependencies, and conventions of the codebase it operates on? This includes framework awareness, directory navigation, and config file comprehension.

sections.heading: Terminal Experience
sections.content: How seamless is the command-line workflow? This covers installation, authentication, configuration, streaming output, error messages, interrupt handling, and integration with existing terminal tools.

sections.heading: Speed
sections.content: Latency from request to response. How does the agent perform under real working conditions, including streaming, caching, and parallel operations?

sections.heading: Documentation
sections.content: Is the documentation clear, complete, and up to date? Can a new user get productive without digging through forums or source code?

sections.heading: Cost
sections.content: What is the real cost of using the agent? This includes API pricing, subscription fees, compute overhead, and indirect costs like time spent fixing incorrect output.

sections.heading: Local vs Cloud
sections.content: Does the agent run locally, in the cloud, or both? How does the deployment model affect privacy, latency, offline capability, and data control?

sections.heading: Open Source
sections.content: Is the agent open source? What is the license? Can it be self-hosted, forked, or audited? How active is the community?

sections.heading: Maintainability
sections.content: How easy is it to keep the agent working over time? This includes update frequency, breaking changes, deprecation policies, and long-term viability.

sections.heading: Review Cycle
sections.content: Active agents are evaluated continuously as part of daily work. Occasional agents are re-evaluated at each significant version change or at least once per quarter. Archived reviews are not actively maintained unless a tool re-enters the workflow.

sections.heading: Classifications
sections.content: Classification Meaning Active Used regularly as part of daily engineering work. Reviews are current and maintained. Occasional Evaluated periodically. May be early in maturity or limited in applicability to current needs. Archived Observations from past use. The tool is no longer in the current workflow. Reviews are preserved for reference.

title: OpenCode — Palmshed
summary: A review of OpenCode based on sustained use through July 2026.

sections.heading: OpenCode
sections.content: This review reflects sustained use through July 2026.

sections.heading: Overview
sections.content: OpenCode is an open-source CLI coding agent designed for terminal-native development. It supports multiple LLM backends, provides file editing and code generation, and operates directly in the terminal alongside existing tools. The project emphasizes simplicity and extensibility. OpenCode terminal interface during a coding session.

sections.heading: Review context
sections.content: Status Active First used November 2025 Last reviewed July 2026 Review scope macOS, CLI usage across web and documentation projects Primary use Daily engineering work, project maintenance, content management Source of assessment Sustained use

sections.heading: Strengths
sections.content: Terminal-native by design. Sits alongside git, build tools, and editors without requiring a separate interface or environment. Multi-model support with configuration that is simple enough to change per session. Useful for switching between cost-effective and capable models depending on the task. The command-line interface is clean and restrained. It does not add visual noise or require learning an interface beyond standard terminal conventions. Open source under a permissive license. The code can be inspected, modified, and self-hosted. Development is active and deliberate. Releases arrive at a steady pace, and new features are added without accumulating unnecessary complexity.

sections.heading: Limitations
sections.content: Repository understanding depends on the context provided. Unlike tools with dedicated indexing pipelines, OpenCode does not build an independent project model. It works well when the relevant context is included, but does not infer project structure on its own. The community is smaller than more established agents. Fewer third-party resources, integrations, and shared configurations are available. Documentation covers setup and core usage clearly. Advanced configuration and extension are less thoroughly documented.

sections.heading: Where it fits
sections.content: OpenCode suits day-to-day coding work where a lightweight, terminal-native agent is preferred over an editor extension or heavyweight platform. It works well for feature implementation, refactoring, debugging, and project maintenance. It is less suited to tasks that require deep repository awareness without explicit context, such as navigating an unfamiliar large codebase for the first time.

sections.heading: Current assessment
sections.content: OpenCode has become a regular part of daily engineering work. It does not attempt to match the feature breadth of larger platforms, and that restraint is part of its strength. It occupies a clear space: a simple, capable CLI agent that stays out of the way and does what it is asked. For the kind of work described above, it is the tool reached for first.

sections.heading: Reference
sections.content: Developer Anomaly Co. Website github.com/opencode-ai/opencode Repository github.com/opencode-ai/opencode License Apache 2.0 Platform macOS, Linux Pricing Free and open source. API usage fees for cloud model providers. Local models at no cost. Version reviewed 0.5.0 Reviewed July 2026

sections.heading: Revision history
sections.content: July 2026 Rewritten from sustained-use perspective. Reclassified from Watching to Active. June 2026 Updated review for version 0.5.0. March 2026 Updated review for version 0.3.0. November 2025 Initial review (version 0.1.0).

title: Antigravity — Palmshed
summary: A review of Antigravity based on sustained use through July 2026.

sections.heading: Antigravity
sections.content: This review reflects sustained use through July 2026.

sections.heading: Overview
sections.content: Antigravity is a lightweight CLI coding agent focused on fast operation and minimal dependencies. The design emphasizes simplicity and composability. It operates directly in the terminal with standard filesystem access and code generation capabilities.

sections.heading: Review context
sections.content: Status Active First used January 2026 Last reviewed July 2026 Review scope macOS, CLI usage, lightweight coding tasks Primary use Quick code generation, scripting, small utilities Source of assessment Sustained use

sections.heading: Strengths
sections.content: Lightweight design with minimal dependencies. Installs and runs without heavy setup. Fast startup and response times. The overhead is low enough that it feels immediate for quick tasks. Clean, focused CLI interface. No unnecessary output or configuration surface. Simple configuration model. Easy to adapt to different workflows without reading extensive documentation.

sections.heading: Limitations
sections.content: Features and reliability are still maturing. The tool is early in its development cycle. Context management is limited. Long sessions or complex multi-file changes require careful prompting. Documentation covers basic usage but leaves advanced scenarios undocumented. Smaller community means fewer shared configurations and integrations.

sections.heading: Where it fits
sections.content: Antigravity suits quick, well-scoped tasks where startup speed and simplicity matter more than deep repository understanding. It is useful for code generation, scripting, and small utilities. It is less suited to complex multi-file refactoring or long-running autonomous sessions that require sustained context.

sections.heading: Current assessment
sections.content: Antigravity occupies a defined niche: a minimal, fast CLI agent for lightweight tasks. It does not compete with larger platforms on feature breadth, and that is not its purpose. For quick, focused work where starting fast matters more than deep context, it serves well. Its place in the workflow reflects what it is designed for, not what it lacks.

sections.heading: Reference
sections.content: Developer Antigravity AI Repository github.com/antigravity-ai/antigravity License Open source Platform macOS, Linux Pricing Free and open source. API usage fees for cloud model providers. Version reviewed 0.4.0 Reviewed July 2026

sections.heading: Revision history
sections.content: July 2026 Rewritten from sustained-use perspective. Reclassified from Watching to Active. June 2026 Updated review for version 0.4.0. March 2026 Updated review for version 0.2.0. January 2026 Initial review (version 0.1.0).

title: Codex — Palmshed
summary: A review of Codex based on sustained use through July 2026.

sections.heading: Codex
sections.content: This review reflects sustained use through July 2026.

sections.heading: Overview
sections.content: Codex is a command-line AI coding tool with sandboxed execution, file editing, and OpenAI model integration. It was designed as a standalone CLI companion to the Cursor editor, focused on quick code generation and prototyping. Codex CLI terminal interface.

sections.heading: Review context
sections.content: Status Active First used October 2024 Last reviewed July 2026 Review scope macOS, single-file code generation and prototyping tasks Primary use Quick code generation, prototyping, well-scoped single-file implementations Source of assessment Sustained use for specific task types

sections.heading: Strengths
sections.content: The terminal interface is clean and well organized. Output formatting is clear without being verbose. Sandboxed command execution provides a safety layer for file operations, making experimentation low-risk. Installation and authentication are simple. The tool is ready to use within minutes. Reliable for single-file edits and straightforward code generation with clear requirements. Response times are fast for the kinds of tasks it handles.

sections.heading: Limitations
sections.content: Repository understanding is limited. Codex does not build a project model and works best when given explicit, self-contained context. Context handling degrades on longer tasks or multi-file changes. Sessions beyond a few turns lose coherence. Model choice is restricted to OpenAI. No support for local models or alternative providers. Configuration options are minimal. Useful for simplicity but limiting for advanced workflows.

sections.heading: Where it fits
sections.content: Codex is suited to quick, well-defined coding tasks where the scope is limited to a single file or a clear, contained problem. It is useful for prototyping, generating boilerplate, and getting rapid answers without leaving the terminal. It is less suited to complex multi-file changes, sustained development sessions, or tasks requiring deep project awareness.

sections.heading: Current assessment
sections.content: Codex occupies a specific, narrow space. It is not a general-purpose coding agent, and it does not try to be. For its intended use — quick, safe, single-file code generation — it performs reliably. It is not reached for by default, but for the tasks it handles, it is the most direct option available.

sections.heading: Reference
sections.content: Developer Cursor / OpenAI Repository github.com/cursor/codex License Proprietary Platform macOS, Linux, Windows Pricing Usage-based through the OpenAI API. Free tier available with limited usage. Version reviewed 0.10.0 Reviewed July 2026

sections.heading: Revision history
sections.content: July 2026 Rewritten using evidence-based template. Reclassified from Useful in specific situations to Active. June 2026 Updated review for version 0.10.0. June 2025 Updated review for version 0.7.0. October 2024 Initial review (version 0.3.0).

title: Gemini CLI — Palmshed
summary: A review of Gemini CLI based on periodic evaluation through July 2026.

sections.heading: Gemini CLI
sections.content: This review reflects periodic evaluation through July 2026.

sections.heading: Overview
sections.content: Gemini CLI is Google's command-line AI coding agent, powered by the Gemini model family. It provides terminal-native code generation, file editing, and shell command execution. A large context window and competitive pricing distinguish it from alternatives. Gemini CLI terminal session showing code generation.

sections.heading: Review context
sections.content: Status Occasional First used April 2025 Last reviewed July 2026 Review scope macOS, periodic evaluation across multiple releases Primary use Periodic assessment of progress and capability Source of assessment Periodic evaluation; documentation for pricing and platform details

sections.heading: Strengths
sections.content: Pricing is very competitive. The free tier has reasonable usage limits and paid rates are significantly lower than comparable alternatives. The context window is large. Substantial codebases can be processed within a single session. Response times are fast, especially for straightforward queries. Integrates with Google Cloud services, which is useful for teams in that ecosystem. Cross-platform support via npm.

sections.heading: Limitations
sections.content: Code quality is inconsistent. The tool performs well on common languages and patterns but struggles with less common languages and frameworks. Repository understanding is limited. Project conventions and nuanced structure are frequently missed. Authentication requires Google Cloud project setup, which adds friction compared to simpler CLI tools. Documentation is improving but still lags behind more established tools, especially for advanced usage. Performance on larger, complex codebases has been less reliable in testing.

sections.heading: Where it fits
sections.content: Gemini CLI is worth evaluating for cost-sensitive development, Google Cloud-native projects, or tasks that benefit from a large context window. It is not yet a daily driver for general-purpose work, but its pricing trajectory and rapid improvement cycle make it worth monitoring.

sections.heading: Current assessment
sections.content: Gemini CLI has improved measurably since its initial release, particularly in model capability and pricing. It has not reached the reliability needed for daily use across a range of projects, but it is checked periodically as development continues. Its large context window and low cost are genuine advantages that may become more significant as the tool matures.

sections.heading: Reference
sections.content: Developer Google Website cloud.google.com/gemini-cli License Proprietary Platform macOS, Linux, Windows Pricing Competitive pricing with generous free tier. Pay-as-you-go for extended usage. Version reviewed 1.2.0 Reviewed July 2026

sections.heading: Revision history
sections.content: July 2026 Rewritten using evidence-based template. June 2026 Updated review for version 1.2.0. November 2025 Updated review for version 0.8.0. April 2025 Initial review (version 0.4.0).

title: Claude Code — Palmshed
summary: A review of Claude Code based on sustained daily use through 2025.

sections.heading: Claude Code
sections.content: This review reflects sustained use through late 2025 and periodic evaluation since.

sections.heading: Overview
sections.content: Claude Code is Anthropic's command-line AI coding agent. It operates directly in the terminal with filesystem access, command execution, and context management. It was used as a primary tool for daily engineering work over an extended period. Claude Code reviewing a multi-file change in the terminal.

sections.heading: Review context
sections.content: Status Occasional First used March 2025 Last reviewed July 2026 Review scope macOS, daily use across TypeScript, Python, and Rust projects through 2025 Primary use General-purpose coding, multi-file refactoring, codebase exploration (historical) Source of assessment Sustained daily use (2025); periodic evaluation since

sections.heading: Strengths
sections.content: Code generation quality is consistently good and respects existing project conventions. Multi-file refactoring works reliably across several files in a single session. Asks clarifying questions when requirements are ambiguous rather than guessing. Repository understanding is strong. It indexes project structure and maintains context well across sessions. The permission system for filesystem and command execution is well designed and provides appropriate guardrails. Streaming output is fast and makes it easy to follow along with the agent's reasoning.

sections.heading: Limitations
sections.content: API costs accumulate quickly with heavy daily use. Usage patterns had to be adjusted to manage expenses. Occasionally over-engineers solutions. A simpler approach would often suffice. Can be verbose in its reasoning output during long sessions, adding noise to the interaction. No meaningful offline mode. All operations require API connectivity. Configuration options are limited compared to open-source alternatives with similar capabilities.

sections.heading: Where it fits
sections.content: Claude Code excels at general-purpose coding across the full stack, particularly in TypeScript, Python, and Rust. It is well suited for feature implementation, refactoring, debugging, and codebase exploration. The cost structure makes it less suitable for high-volume daily use without active cost management.

sections.heading: Current assessment
sections.content: Claude Code was the primary coding agent for much of 2025 and set a standard for repository understanding and code quality. It is no longer the daily driver, but it remains a benchmark against which other tools are measured. The observations here reflect what was learned from extended daily use and continue to inform current evaluations.

sections.heading: Reference
sections.content: Developer Anthropic Website docs.anthropic.com License Proprietary Platform macOS, Linux Pricing API usage-based. Initial credit provided for evaluation. Version reviewed 0.12.0 Reviewed July 2026

sections.heading: Revision history
sections.content: July 2026 Rewritten using evidence-based template. Reclassified from Daily driver to Occasional. June 2026 Updated review for version 0.12.0. November 2025 Updated review for version 0.8.0. March 2025 Initial review (version 0.4.0).

title: Aider — Palmshed
summary: A review of Aider based on sustained use from 2024 through 2025.

sections.heading: Aider
sections.content: This review reflects sustained use from mid-2024 through 2025 and periodic evaluation since.

sections.heading: Overview
sections.content: Aider is an open-source CLI coding assistant that operates directly in the terminal and works with multiple LLM backends, including local and self-hosted models. Its design centers on git-native workflow integration and model flexibility. Aider editing code in a terminal session.

sections.heading: Review context
sections.content: Status Occasional First used June 2024 Last reviewed July 2026 Review scope macOS, sustained use across varied projects (2024–2025) Primary use Cost-sensitive coding, multi-model workflow, local model experimentation Source of assessment Sustained use over approximately 18 months; periodic evaluation since

sections.heading: Strengths
sections.content: Fully open source under Apache 2.0. The code can be inspected, audited, and modified. Works with OpenAI, Anthropic, Google, local, and self-hosted models. This flexibility is valuable for workflows that require switching between cost and capability profiles. The git integration is well designed. Automatic commits with descriptive messages save time and provide a natural safety net. The map-refine architecture provides good repository understanding without consuming excessive context window. Can run entirely locally, which is important for privacy-sensitive projects. Active development community with frequent releases and meaningful improvements.

sections.heading: Limitations
sections.content: Code quality depends heavily on the backend model. This adds variability that is outside the tool's control. Setup requires API key configuration and model selection, which adds initial friction compared to tools with opinionated defaults. The terminal UI is functional but minimal. It lacks refinements found in more polished alternatives. Documentation is scattered across the repository wiki, GitHub issues, and community sources. Edit mistakes occur occasionally and require manual correction, particularly on complex multi-file changes.

sections.heading: Where it fits
sections.content: Aider is well suited for developers who want model flexibility and git-native workflow in an open-source package. It is particularly valuable for cost-sensitive work, local model experimentation, and projects where code auditability matters. The setup friction makes it less suitable for those who prefer opinionated, ready-to-use tools.

sections.heading: Current assessment
sections.content: Aider set a standard for open-source CLI coding agents through its model flexibility and thoughtful git integration. It was a primary tool through much of 2024 and 2025. While no longer the daily driver, it remains a capable tool for specific situations, particularly when model flexibility or local execution is a priority. The architecture decisions it pioneered continue to influence the category.

sections.heading: Reference
sections.content: Developer Paul Gauthier Website aider.chat Repository github.com/paul-gauthier/aider License Apache 2.0 Platform macOS, Linux, Windows Pricing Free and open source. Costs limited to API usage fees. Version reviewed 0.72.0 Reviewed July 2026

sections.heading: Revision history
sections.content: July 2026 Rewritten using evidence-based template. Reclassified from Frequently used to Occasional. June 2026 Updated review for version 0.72.0. September 2025 Updated review for version 0.60.0. June 2024 Initial review (version 0.30.0).

title: Warp — Palmshed
summary: A review of Warp, a terminal emulator with integrated AI features, based on sustained use through 2025.

sections.heading: Warp
sections.content: Status Archived Developer Warp Inc. Version Reviewed 2026.06 Last Updated June 2026 Platform macOS, Linux This tool is no longer in active use. Observations from sustained use through 2025.

sections.heading: Overview
sections.content: Warp is a terminal emulator with integrated AI features: natural-language-to-command translation, smart autocomplete, and workflow automation. Unlike CLI agents that run in any terminal, Warp is a complete terminal replacement that includes AI as a feature rather than being an agent itself. Warp terminal with AI command generation.

sections.heading: Review context
sections.content: Source of assessment: Sustained use from late 2024 through 2025 as primary terminal. No longer in current workflow.

sections.heading: Strengths
sections.content: AI command generation feels natural because it operates within the terminal rather than as a separate interface. Smart autocomplete learns from usage patterns across sessions, improving over time. GPU-accelerated rendering makes it one of the fastest terminals available. Workflows allow saving and sharing multi-step command sequences. Multi-cursor editing, block selection, and fuzzy finder add genuine terminal productivity improvements.

sections.heading: Limitations
sections.content: Not a general-purpose coding agent. AI features are limited to command-line operations and simple scripting. Proprietary terminal. Cannot be used as a headless agent in CI/CD or remote sessions. Commands are sent to Warp servers for AI processing, which matters for sensitive work. Switching from established terminal setups (iTerm2, Kitty, Alacritty) requires adaptation.

sections.heading: Where it fits
sections.content: Warp fills a different category from CLI coding agents. It is a terminal with AI features rather than a standalone agent, making it suitable for interactive local development where command-line efficiency matters. It complements code generation agents rather than replacing them.

sections.heading: Current assessment
sections.content: Warp remains a well-built terminal with genuinely useful AI features for command-line operations. Its value depends on whether the AI features materially improve terminal workflow compared to alternatives. For developers who want AI assistance in the terminal rather than a standalone agent, Warp is worth evaluating.

sections.heading: Reference
sections.content: Warp homepage

sections.heading: Revision history
sections.content: 2026-06 Converted to archived review 2025-08 Updated review for version 2025.08 2024-09 Initial review (version 2024.09)

title: Cursor — Palmshed
summary: A review of Cursor, an AI-first code editor with agent mode, based on sustained use through 2025.

sections.heading: Cursor
sections.content: Status Archived Developer Cursor Inc. Version Reviewed 0.46.x Last Updated June 2026 Platform macOS, Linux, Windows This tool is no longer in active use. Observations from sustained use through 2025.

sections.heading: Overview
sections.content: Cursor is an AI-first code editor built on VS Code. Its agent mode and terminal integration make it relevant as a coding environment, though it is primarily a GUI editor. It supports multiple model backends including Claude and GPT-4. Cursor showing inline AI diffs during a refactoring session.

sections.heading: Review context
sections.content: Source of assessment: Sustained use from late 2024 through 2025 as secondary editor alongside CLI agents. No longer in current workflow.

sections.heading: Strengths
sections.content: Inline editing with diffs makes it easy to review AI suggestions before accepting them. Multi-model support allows choosing the right model for different tasks. Tab-to-accept completions are fast and contextually aware. Composer mode handles multi-file changes effectively within the editor context. Particularly effective for rapid prototyping and exploratory coding sessions.

sections.heading: Limitations
sections.content: Requires the editor to be running. Not a pure CLI tool for headless or remote sessions. Agent mode can be aggressive, making unexpected changes across files. Performance degrades on larger projects, especially during indexing. Code is sent to third-party model providers, which matters for sensitive work. Subscription cost adds to monthly tooling expenses.

sections.heading: Where it fits
sections.content: Cursor is valuable for developers who want AI assistance deeply integrated into their editor. Its diff-based review workflow and multi-file changes make it effective for refactoring. The editor dependency means it complements rather than replaces CLI agents.

sections.heading: Current assessment
sections.content: Cursor remains a polished AI coding environment with meaningful productivity benefits for editor-centric workflows. Its primary advantage is the tight feedback loop between AI suggestions and visual diff review. The subscription cost and editor dependency are the main considerations for adoption.

sections.heading: Reference
sections.content: Cursor homepage

sections.heading: Revision history
sections.content: 2026-06 Converted to archived review 2025-10 Updated review for version 0.42.x 2024-08 Initial review (version 0.30.x)

title: Continue — Palmshed
summary: A review of Continue, an open-source AI code assistant for IDEs, based on evaluation through 2025.

sections.heading: Continue
sections.content: Status Archived Developer Continue Dev Version Reviewed 1.2.0 Last Updated June 2026 Platform VS Code, JetBrains This tool is no longer in active use. Observations from evaluation through 2024 and 2025.

sections.heading: Overview
sections.content: Continue is an open-source AI code assistant that integrates with VS Code and JetBrains. It provides inline code suggestions, chat-based assistance, and custom slash commands. Unlike standalone CLI agents, it operates within the editor and can be configured to use various LLM backends, including local models. Continue chat interface in VS Code.

sections.heading: Review context
sections.content: Source of assessment: Evaluation from mid-2024 through 2025 as an in-editor assistant alongside CLI agents. No longer in current workflow.

sections.heading: Strengths
sections.content: Fully open source under Apache 2.0 license. Code can be inspected and modified. Flexible model backend support, including local models for privacy-sensitive work. Custom slash commands allow creating reusable workflows for common tasks. Active community with a growing plugin ecosystem.

sections.heading: Limitations
sections.content: Requires an IDE. Not a standalone CLI agent for headless or remote use. Context understanding is limited to open files and project index, reducing awareness compared to CLI agents with full filesystem access. Setup is more complex than commercial alternatives, particularly for custom model configurations. Occasional instability when switching models during a session.

sections.heading: Where it fits
sections.content: Continue fills a specific niche as a privacy-respecting, IDE-integrated code assistant with flexible model backends. It is best suited for teams that need to self-host or use custom model deployments, and for developers who want AI assistance within the editor rather than through a separate interface.

sections.heading: Current assessment
sections.content: Continue is a well-designed open-source tool for in-editor AI assistance. Its strength is flexibility and privacy, not autonomous capability. It serves a complementary role to CLI agents rather than replacing them.

sections.heading: Reference
sections.content: Continue homepage

sections.heading: Revision history
sections.content: 2026-06 Converted to archived review 2025-07 Updated review for version 0.8.0 2024-07 Initial review (version 0.5.0)

title: Goose — Palmshed
summary: A review of Goose, an open-source sandboxed AI agent by Block, based on evaluation through early 2026.

sections.heading: Goose
sections.content: Status Archived Developer Block Version Reviewed 1.5.0 Last Updated June 2026 Platform macOS, Linux This tool is no longer in active use. Observations from evaluation in 2025 and early 2026.

sections.heading: Overview
sections.content: Goose is an open-source AI agent developed by Block (formerly Square) that operates in a sandboxed environment. It can edit code, execute terminal commands, and browse the web. The design emphasizes safety and developer control through granular permission management.

sections.heading: Review context
sections.content: Source of assessment: Evaluation from early 2025 through early 2026 for projects requiring sandboxed agent execution. No longer in current workflow.

sections.heading: Strengths
sections.content: Strong safety model with granular permission controls for file and command access. Open source with corporate backing from Block, providing confidence in long-term maintenance. Sandboxed execution environment prevents accidental system modifications. Supports multiple LLM backends including local models. Extensible via MCP. Thorough documentation with practical examples.

sections.heading: Limitations
sections.content: Setup is more involved than simpler CLI agents due to the sandbox and runtime requirements. Sandbox adds noticeable latency compared to agents operating directly on the filesystem. Code generation quality depends heavily on the backend model chosen. Smaller community compared to more established tools. Occasional sandbox stability issues.

sections.heading: Where it fits
sections.content: Goose is best suited for projects where safety and isolation are primary concerns. The sandboxed execution and granular permission model make it appropriate for environments where agent actions need careful containment. The overhead means it is less suitable for fast-paced iterative development.

sections.heading: Current assessment
sections.content: Goose remains a well-designed open-source agent with a strong safety model. Its value is situational: it excels where sandboxed execution and permission control matter, but the overhead limits its appeal for everyday development where speed matters more than isolation.

sections.heading: Reference
sections.content: Goose homepage

title: OpenHands — Palmshed
summary: A review of OpenHands, an open-source platform for autonomous coding agents, based on evaluation through 2025.

sections.heading: OpenHands
sections.content: Status Archived Developer All-Hands-AI Version Reviewed 0.9.0 Last Updated June 2026 Platform macOS, Linux, Web UI This tool is no longer in active use. Observations from evaluation in 2025.

sections.heading: Overview
sections.content: OpenHands (formerly OpenDevin) is an open-source platform for autonomous AI coding agents. It provides both a web interface and command-line access, with agents that can write code, use the terminal, browse the web, and interact with APIs. The project explores the frontier of autonomous agent capabilities. OpenHands web interface showing an autonomous coding session.

sections.heading: Review context
sections.content: Source of assessment: Evaluation from early 2025 through 2025 for experimental autonomous agent workflows. No longer in current workflow.

sections.heading: Strengths
sections.content: Ambitious scope: agents can browse the web, run code, and interact with external services. Open source with an active community contributing regularly. Sandboxed execution environment adds a safety layer for autonomous operations. Plugin architecture extends agent capabilities. Supports multiple LLM backends. Web UI provides visual feedback alongside CLI access.

sections.heading: Limitations
sections.content: Setup and configuration are complex compared to simpler CLI agents. Autonomous agent behavior can be unpredictable and difficult to debug. Resource intensive due to sandbox requirements. CLI experience is less polished than dedicated CLI agents. Code quality varies significantly across models and tasks.

sections.heading: Where it fits
sections.content: OpenHands is appropriate for experimentation with autonomous coding agents and understanding the capabilities and limitations of broad-tool-access AI systems. It is less suitable for production work where reliability and predictability are required.

sections.heading: Current assessment
sections.content: OpenHands demonstrates the potential of autonomous coding agents but has not reached the reliability needed for daily production use. The platform is valuable for research and exploration, particularly for understanding what autonomous agents can achieve with broad tool access.

sections.heading: Reference
sections.content: OpenHands homepage

title: Cline — Palmshed
summary: A review of Cline, an autonomous AI coding assistant for VS Code, based on evaluation through early 2026.

sections.heading: Cline
sections.content: Status Archived Developer Cline Bot Version Reviewed 3.4.0 Last Updated June 2026 Platform VS Code This tool is no longer in active use. Observations from evaluation in 2025 and early 2026.

sections.heading: Overview
sections.content: Cline is a VS Code extension for AI-assisted coding that provides autonomous multi-step task execution. It can read and write files, execute terminal commands, and interact with language servers, operating with agentic behavior within the editor. Cline autonomous coding session in VS Code.

sections.heading: Review context
sections.content: Source of assessment: Evaluation from early 2025 through early 2026 for automated refactoring and code generation within VS Code. No longer in current workflow.

sections.heading: Strengths
sections.content: Autonomous multi-step task execution within the editor, capable of complex workflows. Good integration with VS Code language server protocol for code understanding. Supports multiple LLM providers including OpenAI, Anthropic, and local models. Transparent execution model showing planned actions before execution. Active development with regular updates.

sections.heading: Limitations
sections.content: Tied to VS Code. Not usable in other editors or as a standalone terminal agent. Autonomous behavior can be unpredictable, especially on complex tasks. Resource usage is high during indexing and analysis on larger projects. Code quality varies significantly between different backend models.

sections.heading: Where it fits
sections.content: Cline is appropriate for VS Code users who want an autonomous coding assistant for multi-step tasks within the editor. Its strength is automated refactoring and code generation where the transparent execution model allows reviewing planned actions before they execute.

sections.heading: Current assessment
sections.content: Cline is a capable autonomous coding assistant for VS Code. Its multi-step task execution can be impressive for well-defined tasks. The primary limitations are VS Code dependency and variable reliability on complex or ambiguous tasks.

sections.content: Cline homepage

title: Roo Code — Palmshed
summary: A review of Roo Code, an autonomous AI coding assistant for VS Code, based on evaluation through early 2026.

sections.heading: Roo Code
sections.content: Status Archived Developer Roo Version Reviewed 2.0.0 Last Updated June 2026 Platform VS Code This tool is no longer in active use. Observations from evaluation in 2025 and early 2026.

sections.heading: Overview
sections.content: Roo Code is an AI coding assistant for VS Code that provides autonomous coding capabilities: file creation and editing, terminal command execution, and multi-step development tasks with visible planning. Roo Code interface in VS Code showing task planning.

sections.heading: Review context
sections.content: Source of assessment: Evaluation from early 2025 through early 2026 for automated refactoring and code generation within VS Code. No longer in current workflow.

sections.heading: Strengths
sections.content: Autonomous task execution with visible multi-step planning, providing confidence in agent actions. Good VS Code integration with filesystem and terminal access. Supports multiple model providers for flexibility. Clear visibility into agent planning and execution before changes are applied. Active development with frequent feature updates.

sections.heading: Limitations
sections.content: Tied to VS Code. Not a standalone CLI solution for headless or remote use. Autonomous behavior can be unpredictable on complex or ambiguous tasks. Code quality varies with model choice and task complexity. Resource usage during indexing can impact editor performance on larger projects.

sections.heading: Where it fits
sections.content: Roo Code is suitable for VS Code users seeking an autonomous agent for well-defined multi-step tasks within the editor. The planning visibility makes it appropriate for scenarios where reviewing agent intent before execution is important.

sections.heading: Current assessment
sections.content: Roo Code shows promise for autonomous coding within VS Code, particularly for tasks where planning visibility matters. It has not reached the reliability of more mature CLI agents for complex or ambiguous work.

title: Amp — Palmshed
summary: A review of Amp, a CLI coding agent for fast interactive code generation, based on early evaluation in 2026.

sections.heading: Amp
sections.content: Status Archived Developer Amp AI Version Reviewed 0.3.0 Last Updated June 2026 Platform macOS, Linux This tool is no longer in active use. Observations from early evaluation in 2026.

sections.heading: Overview
sections.content: Amp is a CLI coding agent designed for fast, interactive code generation and editing. The focus is on providing a smooth developer experience with minimal configuration. It was in early development during evaluation.

sections.heading: Review context
sections.content: Source of assessment: Brief evaluation in early 2026. Tool was at an early stage (0.x) and had limited capabilities. No longer in current workflow.

sections.heading: Strengths
sections.content: Interactive and responsive CLI interface with a pleasant developer experience. Fast code generation for common patterns and popular frameworks. Simple setup process with sensible defaults.

sections.heading: Limitations
sections.content: Early stage. Features and stability were still developing. Limited context handling for long sessions. Repository understanding was basic. Small community with limited resources. Documentation was incomplete.

sections.heading: Where it fits
sections.content: Amp was appropriate for quick, simple code generation tasks where speed and low friction mattered. It was not suitable for complex engineering work or production use during the evaluation period.

sections.heading: Current assessment
sections.content: Amp showed promise as an early-stage CLI agent with good developer experience. It was not ready for production use. The project may have matured since this evaluation.

sections.heading: Overview
sections.content: Crush is a CLI coding agent focused on rapid code generation and editing, with an emphasis on simplicity and low friction. It was in very early development (version 0.2.0) during evaluation. Crush CLI interface.

sections.heading: Review context
sections.content: Source of assessment: Brief evaluation in early 2026. Tool was at a very early stage (0.2.0) with minimal features. No longer in current workflow.

sections.heading: Strengths
sections.content: Simple, focused CLI with minimal learning curve. Fast code generation for common programming tasks. Easy installation and setup. Low overhead and small dependency footprint.

sections.heading: Limitations
sections.content: Very early development. Features were minimal with limited language and framework support. No meaningful repository understanding. Small community with minimal resources. Documentation was sparse. Stability issues with complex tasks.

sections.heading: Where it fits
sections.content: Crush was suitable for developers curious about CLI AI agents who wanted a simple, low-commitment tool to experiment with. It was not useful for practical engineering work during the evaluation period.

sections.heading: Current assessment
sections.content: Crush was too early in development to assess seriously. The direction was worth monitoring, but the tool was not yet useful for practical work. The project may have matured since this evaluation.

title: Qwen Code — Palmshed
summary: A review of Qwen Code, Alibaba Cloud's CLI coding agent, based on evaluation through 2025 and 2026.

sections.heading: Qwen Code
sections.content: Status Archived Developer Alibaba Cloud Version Reviewed 2.5.0 Last Updated June 2026 Platform macOS, Linux, Windows This tool is no longer in active use. Observations from evaluation from mid-2025 through early 2026.

sections.heading: Overview
sections.content: Qwen Code is Alibaba Cloud's command-line AI coding agent, built on the Qwen model family. It provides code generation, file editing, and terminal integration. The model quality improved noticeably with each release during the evaluation period. Qwen Code CLI interface.

sections.heading: Review context
sections.content: Source of assessment: Evaluation from mid-2025 through early 2026. Model improved faster than CLI tooling. No longer in current workflow.

sections.heading: Strengths
sections.content: Competitive performance on coding benchmarks, particularly for Python and Java. Very low cost compared to Western alternatives. Open-weight models available for self-hosting. Good support for Chinese-language documentation and comments. Rapid iteration cycle with frequent model updates.

sections.heading: Limitations
sections.content: CLI tooling was immature with rough edges in installation and configuration. Documentation primarily in Chinese. English translations lagged behind. Smaller community and fewer third-party resources. Code quality dropped outside of well-represented languages. Privacy considerations for code sent to Alibaba Cloud servers.

sections.heading: Where it fits
sections.content: Qwen Code is most relevant for developers in the Alibaba Cloud ecosystem or those needing a low-cost coding agent. The open-weight models are interesting for teams that want to self-host.

sections.heading: Current assessment
sections.content: Qwen Code showed rapid progress in model quality and pricing. The CLI tooling was not yet mature enough for daily production use. The project may have matured significantly since this evaluation, particularly in the tooling experience.

sections.content: Qwen Code homepage
